Unnamed: 30: ﻿https://www.sssimonandjude.org/bulletins/ ; info is really limited but the church site mentions, in a header or two, that the church was established in 1830

﻿There are two churches listed in the almanac for the Buffalo Creek area. One of them is not named, and just says 'Buffaloe Creek,' the other is called 'B.V. Nativity' here. I suspect that the Buffaloe Creek entry is referring to something further up the creek, back into the woods north of Freeport, where the church St. Mary's is. Based on the church history given on the St. Mary's (freeport) site, I'm inclined to think that the B.V. Nativity means Blessed Virgin of the Nativity (i.e., Mary). For that reason I'm leaving this Buffaloe Creek entry empty and giving the ID to the church explicity named later on.
﻿https://stmaryfreeport.com/history ; see the Buffaloe Creek notes section for rationale behind the ID assignment here
﻿https://www.stfxcc.org/history ; History-Time Line of STFX Chuch, R. Kenworthy.pdf

affiliated: ﻿under the charge of the Christian Brothers

Unnamed: 30: ﻿This city is in Ohio and, from what I can tell, has never been part of PA, where it appears in the 1833 alamanac. My read on this is that, due to it being in eastern Ohio, closer to Cleveland (which is not yet in this almanac), it was categorized under PA because it was one of their outerlying served areas. For this reason, I've given it a Cleveland ID, as the city appears in the later 1860/70s almanacs.

﻿"In 1828 the Poor Claire nuns came to Pittsburgh and established a convent and academy on Nunnery Hill in Allegheny City -present day North Side of Pittsburgh." (https://pghshrines.org/history-st-stanislaus)

affiliated: ﻿conducted by Les Dames de la Retraite
